content: Newborn & Infant Care Comprehensive Knowledge Base for Parents & Caregivers Compiled and organised from authoritative sources: World Health Organization (WHO) · Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) National Health Service (NHS) · MedlinePlus (NIH) · Mayo Clinic Disclaimer: This document is a structured knowledge base compiled from publicly available health information for educational and RAG-database purposes. It does not constitute medical advice. Always consult a qualified healthcare professional regarding individual health decisions for your baby. Table of Contents 1. Feeding – Breastfeeding – Importance, benefits, technique, supply & support 2. Feeding – Formula & Bottle – Formula types, preparation, bottle hygiene 3. Complementary Feeding & Weaning – Solid foods, timing, textures, finger foods 4. Sleep – Needs by age, routines, coping with night waking 5. Safe Sleep & SIDS Prevention – Positioning, environment, co-sleeping, risk factors 6. Developmental Milestones – CDC milestones: 2 m to 12 m; what to watch for 7. Crying & Soothing – Why babies cry, calming strategies, colic 8. Hygiene & Physical Care – Bathing, nappy care, skin, cord, nails 9. Common Newborn Health Problems – Jaundice, colic, reflux, rashes, colds & more 10. When to Seek Medical Help – Warning signs of serious illness, emergency signs 11. Bonding, Play & Development – Skin-to-skin, tummy time, stimulation, speech 12. Vaccinations – Recommended schedules, why they matter 13. Nutrition Vitamins & Supplements – Vitamin D, A & C, iron, WHO global context 14. Parental Wellbeing – Postnatal support, stress, sleep, asking for help 1. Feeding – Breastfeeding Why Breastfeeding Matters Breast milk is universally recognised as the optimal food for newborns and infants. The World Health Organization (WHO) and UNICEF jointly recommend early initiation of breastfeeding within one hour of birth, exclusive breastfeeding for the first six months of life, and continued breastfeeding alongside appropriate complementary foods up to two years of age or beyond. Globally, only about 44% of infants aged 0–6 months are exclusively breastfed, yet optimal breastfeeding could save more than 820,000 children under five every year. The NHS states that breast milk meets all of a baby's nutritional needs and is available whenever the baby needs it. MedlinePlus confirms that breast milk contains the right balance of nutrients to help an infant grow into a strong and healthy toddler, and that some nutrients in breast milk help protect against common childhood illnesses and infections. Benefits for the Baby Exclusive breastfeeding for six months provides chief protection against gastrointestinal infections in both developing and industrialised countries. Early initiation — within the first hour of birth — protects the newborn from acquiring infections and reduces newborn mortality. Breast milk provides half or more of a child's energy needs between 6 and 12 months, and one third of energy needs between 12 and 24 months. It is also a critical source of energy and nutrients during illness and reduces mortality in malnourished children. The NHS reports that breastfeeding reduces a baby's risk of infections, diarrhoea

metadata.section: Table of Contents > 1. Feeding – Breastfeeding

content: Benefits for the Baby Exclusive breastfeeding for six months provides chief protection against gastrointestinal infections in both developing and industrialised countries. Early initiation — within the first hour of birth — protects the newborn from acquiring infections and reduces newborn mortality. Breast milk provides half or more of a child's energy needs between 6 and 12 months, and one third of energy needs between 12 and 24 months. It is also a critical source of energy and nutrients during illness and reduces mortality in malnourished children. The NHS reports that breastfeeding reduces a baby's risk of infections, diarrhoea and vomiting, sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS), obesity, and cardiovascular disease in adulthood. Children and adolescents who were breastfed as babies are less likely to be overweight or obese, perform better on intelligence tests, and have higher school attendance. Breastfeeding is associated with higher income in adult life. Benefits for the Mother Longer durations of breastfeeding benefit mothers too. It reduces the risk of ovarian and breast cancer, osteoporosis, cardiovascular disease, and obesity. It also helps space pregnancies — exclusive breastfeeding of babies under six months has a hormonal effect that often suppresses menstruation, a natural (though not fail-safe) method of birth control known as the Lactational Amenorrhoea Method (LAM). The First Few Days – Colostrum The fluid breasts produce in the first few days after birth is called colostrum — thick, usually golden-yellow, and highly concentrated. The NHS advises that a baby will only need about a teaspoonful at each feed in these early days. Babies may feed every hour initially, then graduate to fewer but longer feeds as the mother's mature milk comes in, usually around 2–4 days after birth. The more frequently the baby breastfeeds, the more milk is produced (the let-down reflex). It is not possible to overfeed a breastfed baby. Skin-to-skin contact immediately after birth helps keep the baby warm and calm, steadies breathing, and is the ideal time for the first breastfeed. It also helps the baby use natural crawling and latching-on reflexes. (NHS) Feeding Frequency & Hunger Cues Newborns need 8 to 12 feedings per day — approximately one every 2 to 3 hours (Mayo Clinic). In the first week they may want to feed very often, even every hour. The NHS recommends feeding on demand — as often as the child wants, day and night. Early hunger cues include restlessness, sucking on fists or fingers, murmuring sounds, and rooting (turning the head with mouth open). Crying is a late hunger cue; the sooner feeding begins, the less likely the baby will become inconsolably distressed. As babies grow older they take more milk in less time. Building and Maintaining Milk Supply Each time a baby feeds, the body is signalled to produce more milk for the next feed. It is important to breastfeed at night, as prolactin (the hormone that builds supply) is produced in greater quantities at night.

metadata.section: Table of Contents > 1. Feeding – Breastfeeding > (LAM).

content: murmuring sounds, and rooting (turning the head with mouth open). Crying is a late hunger cue; the sooner feeding begins, the less likely the baby will become inconsolably distressed. As babies grow older they take more milk in less time. Building and Maintaining Milk Supply Each time a baby feeds, the body is signalled to produce more milk for the next feed. It is important to breastfeed at night, as prolactin (the hormone that builds supply) is produced in greater quantities at night. The NHS warns that in the early weeks, 'topping up' with formula or giving a dummy can lower supply. If there are concerns about supply, a midwife, health visitor, or lactation consultant should be contacted. The National Breastfeeding Helpline in the UK is available 24 hours a day on 0300 100 0212. MedlinePlus advises contacting a lactation consultant if problems arise. HIV and Infant Feeding Antiretroviral drugs (ART) now allow mothers living with HIV to breastfeed with significantly reduced risk of transmission. WHO recommends all people living with HIV take ART for life from the time they first learn their infection status. Mothers in settings where diarrhoea, pneumonia, and malnutrition remain prevalent should exclusively breastfeed for 6 months and continue up to at least 12 months. Sources: WHO Infant and Young Child Feeding Fact Sheet (Dec 2023); NHS – Benefits of Breastfeeding; NHS – Breastfeeding: The First Few Days; MedlinePlus – Breastfeeding; Mayo Clinic – Feeding Your Newborn 2. Feeding – Formula & Bottle Feeding When to Use Formula When breastfeeding is not possible, first infant formula is the only suitable alternative for babies under 12 months. Mayo Clinic advises that healthy newborns do not need cereal, water, juice, or other fluids. The NHS states that 'follow-on' formula is not suitable for babies under 6 months, and is not necessary after 6 months either. Other milks — cows', goats', or sheep's milk — should not be introduced as a main drink until 12 months of age. Cows' milk can be used in cooking or mixed with food from around 6 months. Formula Does Not Replicate Breast Milk The NHS is clear that formula milk does not provide the same protection from illness as breast milk and does not give the mother any health benefits. However, when breastfeeding is not possible, properly prepared formula provides adequate nutrition for healthy growth and development. Bottle Hygiene and Preparation MedlinePlus provides guidance on buying and caring for baby bottles and nipples. Key hygiene principles when preparing formula include: always washing hands before preparing formula; sterilising all bottles, teats, and caps; using freshly boiled (then cooled) water; measuring formula powder accurately; and checking the temperature of the feed before giving it to the baby. Prepared formula should be stored in the refrigerator and used within 24 hours. Leftover formula from a feed should be discarded. Mayo Clinic advises making feedings routine, and involving all caregivers in using the same approach. Bonding During Bottle Feeding

metadata.section: Table of Contents > 1. Feeding – Breastfeeding > (LAM). > 2. Feeding – Formula & Bottle Feeding

content: Bottle Hygiene and Preparation MedlinePlus provides guidance on buying and caring for baby bottles and nipples. Key hygiene principles when preparing formula include: always washing hands before preparing formula; sterilising all bottles, teats, and caps; using freshly boiled (then cooled) water; measuring formula powder accurately; and checking the temperature of the feed before giving it to the baby. Prepared formula should be stored in the refrigerator and used within 24 hours. Leftover formula from a feed should be discarded. Mayo Clinic advises making feedings routine, and involving all caregivers in using the same approach. Bonding During Bottle Feeding Mayo Clinic emphasises that feeding time — whether breast or bottle — is an opportunity to bond with the baby. Caregivers should hold the baby close, make eye contact, talk in a gentle voice, and use each feeding to build the baby's sense of safety, trust, and comfort. MedlinePlus also references guidance on burping your baby after feeds to release trapped wind. Sources: NHS – Baby's First Solid Foods (0–6 months feeding); NHS – How to Bottle Feed; MedlinePlus – Infant and Newborn Care; Mayo Clinic – Feeding Your Newborn 3. Complementary Feeding & Weaning (Starting Solid Foods) When to Start Solids Both WHO and the NHS recommend that complementary (solid) foods should be introduced at around 6 months of age, alongside continued breastfeeding or formula. The NHS explains that waiting until around 6 months gives the baby's digestive system time to develop, helps protect against illness and infections if breastfeeding continues, and allows the baby to develop the physical ability to move food around the mouth, chew, and swallow. WHO states that if complementary foods are not introduced around 6 months, or if they are given inappropriately, an infant's growth may falter. Signs of Readiness for Solid Foods The NHS identifies three clear signs that, when appearing together from around 6 months, indicate readiness: the ability to sit in a stable position and hold the head steady; the ability to co-ordinate eyes, hands, and mouth to pick up food and put it in their mouth; and the ability to swallow food rather than spit it out. Chewing fists, waking more at night, or wanting extra milk feeds are not reliable indicators of readiness for solids and should not be used as reasons to start early. WHO Guiding Principles for Complementary Feeding • Continue frequent, on-demand breastfeeding until 2 years of age or beyond. • Practise responsive feeding — feed infants directly; feed slowly and patiently; encourage them to eat but do not force; talk to the child and maintain eye contact. • Practise good hygiene and proper food handling. • Start at 6 months with small amounts and increase gradually as the child gets older. • Gradually increase food consistency and variety. • Increase meals to 2–3 per day for infants 6–8 months, and 3–4 per day for 9–23 months, with 1–2 additional snacks as required. • Use fortified complementary foods or vitamin-mineral supplements as needed.

metadata.section: Table of Contents > 1. Feeding – Breastfeeding > (LAM). > 2. Feeding – Formula & Bottle Feeding > 3. Complementary Feeding & Weaning (Starting Solid Foods)

content: • Practise responsive feeding — feed infants directly; feed slowly and patiently; encourage them to eat but do not force; talk to the child and maintain eye contact. • Practise good hygiene and proper food handling. • Start at 6 months with small amounts and increase gradually as the child gets older. • Gradually increase food consistency and variety. • Increase meals to 2–3 per day for infants 6–8 months, and 3–4 per day for 9–23 months, with 1–2 additional snacks as required. • Use fortified complementary foods or vitamin-mineral supplements as needed. • During illness: increase fluid intake including breastfeeding, and offer soft favourite foods. First Foods (NHS) The NHS recommends starting with single vegetables and fruits such as mashed or soft cooked sticks of parsnip, broccoli, potato, sweet potato, carrot, apple, or pear. Including vegetables that are not sweet (such as broccoli, cauliflower, and spinach) helps babies get used to a range of flavours and may reduce fussy eating later. Foods containing allergens — including peanuts, hens' eggs, gluten, and fish — can be introduced from around 6 months, one at a time and in small amounts to spot any reaction. Salt and sugar must not be added to baby food or cooking water. Babies should not eat salty food as it is not good for their kidneys, and sugar can cause tooth decay. Texture Progression The NHS advises moving babies from puréed or blended foods to mashed, lumpy, or finger foods as soon as they can manage them. This helps them learn to chew, move food around their mouth, and swallow. Some babies are happy to start with mashed or lumpy food from the beginning. Finger food — pieces about the size of a finger that can be gripped in a fist — should be offered at each meal from the start to encourage self-feeding and develop fine motor skills. Hard foods like whole nuts, raw carrot, and raw apple should be avoided due to choking risk. Small round foods like grapes and cherry tomatoes must be cut into quarters. Baby-Led Weaning Baby-led weaning means giving a baby only finger foods and letting them feed themselves from the start instead of using puréed or mashed food on a spoon. The NHS states there is no right or wrong approach — some parents prefer it, others use a combination. There is no increased choking risk when a baby self-feeds compared to spoon feeding, provided appropriate foods are offered. The most important outcome is that the baby eats a wide variety of food and gets all the nutrients needed. Meal Frequency by Age (NHS) 6 months: Start with a small amount of food before the usual milk feed. 7–9 months: Gradually increase towards 3 meals a day (breakfast, lunch, tea), alongside around 4 milk feeds per day; formula-fed babies may need around 600 ml of milk per day. 10–12 months: 3 meals a day plus approximately 3 milk feeds;

metadata.section: Table of Contents > 1. Feeding – Breastfeeding > (LAM). > 2. Feeding – Formula & Bottle Feeding > 3. Complementary Feeding & Weaning (Starting Solid Foods)

content: self-feeds compared to spoon feeding, provided appropriate foods are offered. The most important outcome is that the baby eats a wide variety of food and gets all the nutrients needed. Meal Frequency by Age (NHS) 6 months: Start with a small amount of food before the usual milk feed. 7–9 months: Gradually increase towards 3 meals a day (breakfast, lunch, tea), alongside around 4 milk feeds per day; formula-fed babies may need around 600 ml of milk per day. 10–12 months: 3 meals a day plus approximately 3 milk feeds; formula-fed babies around 400 ml daily. Cows' milk should not be given as a main drink until 12 months. An open cup or free-flow cup without a valve should be introduced at around 6 months. Sources: WHO – Infant and Young Child Feeding Fact Sheet; NHS – Your Baby's First Solid Foods; NHS – Weaning and Feeding 4. Sleep How Much Sleep Does a Baby Need? Mayo Clinic notes that sleep is the brain's main activity for newborns — babies spend about half of their sleeping time in REM (dream) sleep, which supports memory, nervous system development, and self-control. The NHS provides these approximate daily sleep totals (including naps): Newborns — around 18 hours total but only 1–2 hours at a stretch; 3–6 months — fewer night feeds, some babies sleep 5–8 hours at a stretch; 6–12 months — around 15 hours total, mostly at night; 12 months onwards — around 12–15 hours total; 2-year-olds — about 12–14 hours including naps; 3–5 year-olds — about 12 hours. Mayo Clinic adds that by 3–4 months many babies sleep at least 5 hours at a time, and at some point during the first year they begin sleeping about 10 hours each night. Distinguishing Night from Day The NHS advises teaching babies from the start that night-time is different from daytime. During the day: open curtains, play games, and allow normal household noise. At night: keep lights dim, speak quietly, put the baby down as soon as they have been fed and changed, and avoid play. Babies will gradually learn that night-time is for sleeping. Mayo Clinic explains that newborns have no natural sense of night and day — a more regular sleep schedule develops with brain and nervous system growth, and as the time between feeds increases. Establishing a Bedtime Routine A simple, soothing bedtime routine helps babies settle and provides valuable one-to-one bonding time. The NHS suggests: a warm bath; changing into night clothes and a fresh nappy; a bedtime story; dimming the lights; a goodnight kiss and cuddle; a lullaby or gentle music. As the child gets older, keeping a similar routine and avoiding excitement just before bed is beneficial. Mayo Clinic recommends putting babies to bed drowsy but awake, which helps link bed with the process of falling asleep. Helping Baby Sleep Through the Night Mayo Clinic offers the following evidence-based tips: (1) Follow a calming bedtime routine — keep the

metadata.section: Table of Contents > 1. Feeding – Breastfeeding > (LAM). > 2. Feeding – Formula & Bottle Feeding > 3. Complementary Feeding & Weaning (Starting Solid Foods) > 4. Sleep

content: The NHS suggests: a warm bath; changing into night clothes and a fresh nappy; a bedtime story; dimming the lights; a goodnight kiss and cuddle; a lullaby or gentle music. As the child gets older, keeping a similar routine and avoiding excitement just before bed is beneficial. Mayo Clinic recommends putting babies to bed drowsy but awake, which helps link bed with the process of falling asleep. Helping Baby Sleep Through the Night Mayo Clinic offers the following evidence-based tips: (1) Follow a calming bedtime routine — keep the bedroom quiet and lights low, and avoid play just before sleep. (2) Put the baby to bed drowsy but awake when early signs of tiredness appear (rubbing eyes). (3) Give the baby time to settle — allow a few minutes before checking on the baby if fussing; some babies fall asleep faster if left briefly. (4) Swaddling can help some babies settle, but should stop once the baby shows signs of rolling (around 4 months). (5) A pacifier at sleep time may help and is associated with reduced SIDS risk. Newborns must always be placed on their backs to sleep unless a health professional advises otherwise. Coping with Disturbed Nights The NHS advises parents to sleep when their baby sleeps. Partners should share night feeds and early-morning nappy changes where possible. If formula feeding, partners can take turns. Friends or relatives can be asked to stay for a few days to allow parents to catch up on sleep. Mayo Clinic notes that in the first months caregivers often report exhaustion, and suggests setting aside non-essential tasks and reaching out to a support network. Growth spurts, teething, and illnesses all temporarily disrupt sleep patterns — adjusting routines accordingly is normal. Sources: NHS – Helping Your Baby to Sleep; Mayo Clinic – Helping Baby Sleep Through the Night 5. Safe Sleep & SIDS Prevention What Is SIDS? Sudden Infant Death Syndrome (SIDS) — also historically called cot death — is the sudden and unexplained death of an apparently healthy baby aged up to 12 months. The NHS states it is rare but does still happen. The exact cause is unknown. SIDS is most likely in the first 6 months of life and can happen any time a baby is asleep, day or night. Premature babies and low-birth-weight babies are at higher risk. Mayo Clinic notes that having a baby sleep in the same room as caregivers (in a separate cot) for at least 6 months — ideally up to a year — may help lower SIDS risk. Safe Sleep Dos (NHS) • Always place your baby on their back to sleep, in the 'feet-to-foot' position (feet at the bottom of the cot). • Use a firm, flat mattress in a cot, Moses basket, or pram. • Keep the baby in the same room as you for at least the first 6 months, day and night. • Move the baby onto their back if they roll — until they can roll both ways independently.

metadata.section: Table of Contents > 1. Feeding – Breastfeeding > (LAM). > 2. Feeding – Formula & Bottle Feeding > 3. Complementary Feeding & Weaning (Starting Solid Foods) > 4. Sleep > 5. Safe Sleep & SIDS Prevention

content: 6 months — ideally up to a year — may help lower SIDS risk. Safe Sleep Dos (NHS) • Always place your baby on their back to sleep, in the 'feet-to-foot' position (feet at the bottom of the cot). • Use a firm, flat mattress in a cot, Moses basket, or pram. • Keep the baby in the same room as you for at least the first 6 months, day and night. • Move the baby onto their back if they roll — until they can roll both ways independently. • Breastfeed if possible — breast milk protects against infections that may increase SIDS risk. • Consider a dummy at sleep time — some research suggests this reduces SIDS risk. • Keep vaccinations up to date — this protects against illnesses associated with SIDS. • Keep room temperature between 16–20°C; use a room thermometer to monitor this. Safe Sleep Don'ts (NHS) • Do not sleep on a sofa or armchair with your baby — this is particularly dangerous. • Do not smoke when pregnant or around the baby, and do not allow anyone to smoke in the same room as the baby. • Do not share a bed with the baby if they were born prematurely or weighed less than 2.5 kg at birth. • Do not share a bed if you or your partner smoke, have had 2+ units of alcohol, have taken recreational drugs, or have taken drowsy-making medication. • Do not allow the baby to get too hot or too cold — remove hats and extra clothing as soon as you come indoors. • Do not leave the baby in a car seat for too long — maximum 30 minutes for newborns, 2 hours for older babies. • Do not use pillows, duvets, cot bumpers, or anything that could cover the baby's face. If You Do Share a Bed (Co-sleeping Safety) The NHS strongly advises that the safest place is always a separate cot. However, if bed-sharing occurs: the baby should sleep on their back on a firm, flat mattress; pillows, sheets, and blankets must be kept away from the baby; the baby should not be swaddled in bed (a baby sleeping bag is safer); no other children, babies, or pets should be in the bed; never leave the baby alone in an adult bed; and ensure the baby cannot fall out or become trapped between the mattress and wall. Safe Use of Baby Slings (T.I.C.K.S. Rules – NHS) • Tight — keep the sling tight enough to hug your baby close to your body. • In view — always be able to see your baby's face at all times. • Close — close enough to kiss your baby's head. • Keep chin up — keep your baby's chin off their chest. • Support — support your baby's back in a comfortably upright position. I Never feed a baby while they are in a sling. Never cover the baby's head in a sling.

metadata.section: Table of Contents > 1. Feeding – Breastfeeding > (LAM). > 2. Feeding – Formula & Bottle Feeding > 3. Complementary Feeding & Weaning (Starting Solid Foods) > 4. Sleep > 5. Safe Sleep & SIDS Prevention

content: Safe Use of Baby Slings (T.I.C.K.S. Rules – NHS) • Tight — keep the sling tight enough to hug your baby close to your body. • In view — always be able to see your baby's face at all times. • Close — close enough to kiss your baby's head. • Keep chin up — keep your baby's chin off their chest. • Support — support your baby's back in a comfortably upright position. I Never feed a baby while they are in a sling. Never cover the baby's head in a sling. Sources: NHS – Sudden Infant Death Syndrome (SIDS); Mayo Clinic – Helping Baby Sleep Through the Night; MedlinePlus – Safe Sleep 6. Developmental Milestones Skills such as taking a first step, smiling for the first time, and waving 'bye-bye' are called developmental milestones. Children reach milestones in how they play, learn, speak, act, and move. The CDC's 'Learn the Signs. Act Early.' programme provides milestone checklists from 2 months through 5 years. MedlinePlus explains that normal development can be broken into: gross motor (head control, sitting, crawling, walking); fine motor (gripping a spoon, pincer grasp); sensory (seeing, hearing, tasting, touching, smelling); language (sounds, words, understanding); and social (playing with family and other children). Every baby develops at their own pace — being slightly ahead in some areas and slightly behind in others is entirely normal. Birth to 3 Months (Mayo Clinic) Motor skills: Newborn movements are jerky at first but gradually become more controlled. By 2 months most babies can support their own head when held; by 3 months many can lift the head and chest while on their tummy, supported by their elbows. Babies discover their hands — by 3 months they can grasp toys and bring them to the mouth. Hearing: Newborns can hear but don't yet understand sounds; by 1 month babies recognise familiar sounds; by 3 months they may respond with excitement or quiet to listen to a parent's voice. Vision: In the first 3 months babies pay most attention to faces; they gradually gain the ability to follow a moving object and focus at greater distances; around 2 months babies begin to smile in response to smiles; by 3 months they make eye contact and begin to distinguish colours. Communication: Babies communicate primarily by crying at first; by 2 months they may coo and repeat vowel sounds; by 3 months they begin testing squeaks, growling, and blowing raspberries, and may imitate sounds. By 2 Months (CDC) • Calms down when spoken to or picked up. • Looks at a face and smiles back when someone smiles at them. • Makes sounds other than crying. • Reacts to loud sounds. • Watches things as they move. • Holds head up when on tummy for short periods. By 4 Months (CDC) • Smiles on their own to get attention. • Coos or makes sounds when happy. • Holds head steady without support. • Holds a toy when placed in their hand.

metadata.section: Table of Contents > 1. Feeding – Breastfeeding > (LAM). > 2. Feeding – Formula & Bottle Feeding > 3. Complementary Feeding & Weaning (Starting Solid Foods) > 4. Sleep > 5. Safe Sleep & SIDS Prevention > 6. Developmental Milestones

content: months they begin testing squeaks, growling, and blowing raspberries, and may imitate sounds. By 2 Months (CDC) • Calms down when spoken to or picked up. • Looks at a face and smiles back when someone smiles at them. • Makes sounds other than crying. • Reacts to loud sounds. • Watches things as they move. • Holds head up when on tummy for short periods. By 4 Months (CDC) • Smiles on their own to get attention. • Coos or makes sounds when happy. • Holds head steady without support. • Holds a toy when placed in their hand. • Uses both hands to reach for a toy. • Follows moving objects with their eyes from side to side. By 6 Months (CDC) • Knows familiar faces and begins to know if someone is a stranger. • Likes to play with others, especially parents. • Rolls from tummy to back and back to tummy. • Sits with support. • Passes things from one hand to the other. • Makes squealing noises and strings vowels together when babbling. • Responds to own name. By 9 Months (CDC) • May be afraid of strangers and clings to familiar adults. • Shows several facial expressions (happy, sad, angry, surprised). • Makes a lot of different sounds like 'mamamama' and 'babababa'. • Lifts arms up to be picked up. • Sits without support; pulls to stand; may start crawling. • Picks up small objects with thumb and forefinger (pincer grasp developing). • Looks for objects dropped out of sight. By 12 Months / 1 Year (CDC & Mayo Clinic) • Shy or nervous with strangers; cries when parent leaves the room. • Copies gestures — waves bye-bye, claps hands. • Calls a parent 'mama' or 'dada' and uses exclamations like 'uh-oh'. • Responds to simple verbal requests and understands 'no'. • Pulls up to stand; may take first steps without support. • Uses fingers to feed themselves; may try to use a spoon. • Finds hidden objects easily (object permanence). • Imitates what others do (e.g. pressing buttons, 'talking' on phone). • Improved hand-eye coordination — can bang blocks together, place objects in containers. When to Seek Help (CDC) Parents should act early if their child is not meeting one or more milestones, has lost skills they previously had, or if parents have any other concerns. The American Academy of Pediatrics recommends developmental screening at 9, 18, and 30 months, and autism screening at 18 and 24 months (or whenever there is a concern). If the doctor shares the concern: ask for a referral to a specialist; and contact the state or territory's early intervention programme. The CDC Milestone Tracker mobile app and downloadable checklists are free tools to support tracking. Sources: CDC – Learn the Signs. Act Early. (milestones index); Mayo Clinic – Infant Development: Birth to 3 Months; Mayo Clinic – Milestones 10–12 Months; MedlinePlus – Infant and Newborn Development 7. Crying & Soothing Your Baby Why Babies Cry

content: developmental screening at 9, 18, and 30 months, and autism screening at 18 and 24 months (or whenever there is a concern). If the doctor shares the concern: ask for a referral to a specialist; and contact the state or territory's early intervention programme. The CDC Milestone Tracker mobile app and downloadable checklists are free tools to support tracking. Sources: CDC – Learn the Signs. Act Early. (milestones index); Mayo Clinic – Infant Development: Birth to 3 Months; Mayo Clinic – Milestones 10–12 Months; MedlinePlus – Infant and Newborn Development 7. Crying & Soothing Your Baby Why Babies Cry All babies cry — Mayo Clinic states it is typical for a newborn to cry 1 to 4 hours a day. Crying is a baby's primary way of communicating need. Mayo Clinic explains that in time caregivers may begin to recognise different cries: a hungry cry often comes right after waking and may turn into an angry wail if the baby waits long; a pain cry may sound panicked or shrieking. The NHS identifies the most common reasons as: hunger; a dirty or wet nappy; tiredness; wanting a cuddle; wind (trapped gas); being too hot or too cold; boredom; and overstimulation. Crying typically peaks at around 4 to 8 weeks of age and then gradually decreases. What Babies May Be Communicating (Mayo Clinic) • 'I'm hungry.' Look for early cues: hands moving to the mouth, lip smacking. • 'I want to suck.' Offer a pacifier if the baby isn't hungry. • 'I want to be held.' Hold to shoulder or chest; gently rub or pat the back. • 'I'm tired.' Newborns can sleep around 16 hours a day. • 'I'm wet.' Check and change the nappy. • 'I want to move.' Try gentle rocking, a walk in a carrier, or an infant swing. • 'I'd rather be bundled.' Swaddling helps some babies feel secure. • 'I'm hot or cold.' Dress the baby in as many layers as you are wearing. • 'I'm overwhelmed.' Move to a calmer space; try white noise. Calming Techniques (NHS) • Let the baby suckle at the breast if breastfeeding. • Gentle background noise (music, radio) can distract a crying baby. • Hold the baby or use a sling — move gently, sway, dance, talk, and sing. • Rock the baby in a pram or go for a walk or drive. • Offer something interesting to look at or listen to (rattle, mobile). • Stroke the baby's back firmly and rhythmically. • Gentle baby massage — avoid oils for the first month. • Offer a warm bath. • Try simply laying the baby down after a feed if rocking is keeping them awake. Colic Colic describes intense, frequent, long-lasting crying in an otherwise healthy baby. Mayo Clinic states it often starts a few weeks after birth and typically gets better by 3 to 4 months. Colic may involve crying for 3

metadata.section: Table of Contents > 1. Feeding – Breastfeeding > (LAM). > 2. Feeding – Formula & Bottle Feeding > 3. Complementary Feeding & Weaning (Starting Solid Foods) > 4. Sleep > 5. Safe Sleep & SIDS Prevention > 6. Developmental Milestones > 7. Crying & Soothing Your Baby

content: • Offer something interesting to look at or listen to (rattle, mobile). • Stroke the baby's back firmly and rhythmically. • Gentle baby massage — avoid oils for the first month. • Offer a warm bath. • Try simply laying the baby down after a feed if rocking is keeping them awake. Colic Colic describes intense, frequent, long-lasting crying in an otherwise healthy baby. Mayo Clinic states it often starts a few weeks after birth and typically gets better by 3 to 4 months. Colic may involve crying for 3 or more hours a day on 3 or more days of the week. The cause is not fully understood. The NHS notes the crying may sound distressed, stopping and starting in a pattern that suggests waves of stomach pain. There may be little that can be done except comfort the baby and wait for the episode to pass. MedlinePlus lists colic as a common newborn problem alongside diaper rash, fever, and vomiting. Letting Baby Cry Mayo Clinic states it is acceptable to let a baby cry if they do not seem sick and everything has been tried. Leaving the baby safely in a crib for 10–15 minutes may help; many babies need to cry briefly before they can fall asleep. Never respond to crying by feeling it is a failure of caregiving — sometimes there is nothing to do but be present. I NEVER SHAKE A BABY. Shaking moves the head violently and can cause severe brain damage. If overwhelmed, place the baby safely in their cot, close the door, and take a break to calm yourself before returning. Crying as a Sign of Illness The NHS advises contacting a health visitor, GP, or NHS 111 if: the baby cries constantly and cannot be consoled; the cry does not sound like their normal cry; or if the baby has other symptoms such as a high temperature. Sources: NHS – Soothing a Crying Baby; Mayo Clinic – Crying Baby: What to Do When Your Newborn Cries; MedlinePlus – Common Infant and Newborn Problems 8. Hygiene & Physical Care Topping and Tailing The NHS advises that newborns do not need a full bath every day. 'Topping and tailing' — cleaning the face, neck, hands, and bottom with warm water and cotton wool — is sufficient on other days. The NHS recommends always using a fresh piece of cotton wool for each eye, wiping from the nose outward, to prevent transferring infection from one eye to the other. The baby's ears should be cleaned around — never inside — and cotton buds must never be used inside the ears. Bathing Your Baby (NHS) When giving a full bath: make sure the room is warm; run cold water first then add hot; check temperature with a wrist or elbow; keep the water warm, not hot. Hold the baby securely — one hand supporting the upper arm and head/shoulders, the other gently swishing water over the baby without splashing. Always

Safe Sleep & SIDS Prevention > 6. Developmental Milestones > 7. Crying & Soothing Your Baby > 8. Hygiene & Physical Care

content: prevent transferring infection from one eye to the other. The baby's ears should be cleaned around — never inside — and cotton buds must never be used inside the ears. Bathing Your Baby (NHS) When giving a full bath: make sure the room is warm; run cold water first then add hot; check temperature with a wrist or elbow; keep the water warm, not hot. Hold the baby securely — one hand supporting the upper arm and head/shoulders, the other gently swishing water over the baby without splashing. Always keep the baby's head clear of the water. After the bath, lift the baby out and pat dry, paying special attention to skin folds. NEVER leave a baby alone in the bath, even for a second, and do not use baby bath seats (they can come away from the bath; babies can drown in very little water). Baby massage: After the bath is a good time to massage your baby — this can help them relax and sleep. Check that any oils or lotions are suitable for babies. Avoid oils on newborns under 1 month old. (NHS) Nappy Changes (NHS) Nappies should be changed as soon as possible when wet or dirty to prevent nappy rash. The nappy area should be cleaned with water or fragrance-free, alcohol-free baby wipes. When cleaning girls, always wipe from front to back to prevent infection. The skin should be patted or gently rubbed dry — including between skin folds — before putting on a fresh nappy. Nappies should fit properly and not be too tight. Nappy Rash (NHS) Nappy rash is common and usually manageable at home. Symptoms include red or raw patches on the bottom or nappy area, skin that feels hot to touch, scaly or dry skin, spots or blisters, and a baby that seems uncomfortable. Prevention and treatment: change wet or dirty nappies as soon as possible; leave nappies off when possible; use extra-absorbent nappies; clean with water or fragrance-free wipes; bathe daily (not more than twice a day). A pharmacist can recommend a barrier cream. Do not use soap, baby lotion, bubble bath, talcum powder, or antiseptics on nappy rash. See a GP if the rash does not improve, spreads, or if the baby has a high temperature. Nail Care (NHS) Newborn nails grow quickly and can scratch the baby's face. Trim using baby nail scissors or a fine emery board. It is easiest to cut nails when the baby is asleep or relaxed. Avoid biting nails off, as this can introduce bacteria. Umbilical Cord Care (MedlinePlus) The umbilical cord stump requires specific care until it falls off — typically within 1 to 3 weeks after birth. The NHS advises only cleaning around the cord if it is dirty with urine or faeces: use fresh cotton wool and warm water, and gently pat dry. The area should be kept dry. If the skin around the stump becomes red,

content: board. It is easiest to cut nails when the baby is asleep or relaxed. Avoid biting nails off, as this can introduce bacteria. Umbilical Cord Care (MedlinePlus) The umbilical cord stump requires specific care until it falls off — typically within 1 to 3 weeks after birth. The NHS advises only cleaning around the cord if it is dirty with urine or faeces: use fresh cotton wool and warm water, and gently pat dry. The area should be kept dry. If the skin around the stump becomes red, swollen, or begins to ooze, medical advice should be sought, as this may indicate infection. Head Shape (MedlinePlus) Newborn head moulding — an elongated or misshapen head after vaginal birth — is normal and usually resolves within a few days. The soft spots (fontanelles) are areas where skull bones have not yet fused; they are protected by a tough membrane. A sunken or bulging fontanelle can be a sign of illness and should be assessed by a doctor. Sources: NHS – Washing and Bathing Your Baby; NHS – Nappy Rash; NHS – How to Change Your Baby's Nappy; MedlinePlus – Bathing an Infant; MedlinePlus – Umbilical Cord Care; MedlinePlus – Newborn Head Moulding 9. Common Newborn Health Problems MedlinePlus states that common health problems in babies include colds, coughs, fevers, vomiting, and skin conditions such as nappy rash and cradle cap. Many of these are not serious. It is important to know how to help a sick baby and to recognise warning signs of more serious illness. Trust your intuition — if worried, call a healthcare provider. Jaundice Jaundice (yellowing of the skin and eyes) is very common in newborns in the first week of life, caused by a build-up of bilirubin. MedlinePlus references CDC guidance on jaundice and breastfeeding. Most cases resolve on their own, but severe jaundice requires medical treatment (phototherapy). Parents should monitor for yellowing that spreads, a very yellow or orange colour, difficulty waking the baby, or poor feeding. Colic As discussed in Section 7, colic is a condition of intense, frequent crying in an otherwise healthy baby. MedlinePlus lists colic as a common newborn problem. The NHS describes it as possible waves of stomach cramps. It typically resolves by 3–4 months. A healthcare provider can give the baby a check-up and offer additional soothing advice. Reflux and Spitting Up Spitting up milk after feeds (reflux) is very common in babies and often resolves without treatment. Mayo Clinic covers this under 'Spitting Up in Babies: What's OK, What's Not' — listed in MedlinePlus references. The NHS mentions reflux as a possible cause of crying during feeds. Seek medical advice if the spitting up is forceful (projectile), if there is blood in the spit-up, or if the baby is not gaining weight. Nappy Rash and Cradle Cap Both are mentioned by MedlinePlus as very common. Nappy rash is covered in detail in Section 8. Cradle

Safe Sleep & SIDS Prevention > 6. Developmental Milestones > 7. Crying & Soothing Your Baby > 8. Hygiene & Physical Care > (NHS) > 9. Common Newborn Health Problems

content: Reflux and Spitting Up Spitting up milk after feeds (reflux) is very common in babies and often resolves without treatment. Mayo Clinic covers this under 'Spitting Up in Babies: What's OK, What's Not' — listed in MedlinePlus references. The NHS mentions reflux as a possible cause of crying during feeds. Seek medical advice if the spitting up is forceful (projectile), if there is blood in the spit-up, or if the baby is not gaining weight. Nappy Rash and Cradle Cap Both are mentioned by MedlinePlus as very common. Nappy rash is covered in detail in Section 8. Cradle cap (seborrhoeic dermatitis of the scalp) is a scaly, greasy condition that appears on newborn scalps. It is harmless and usually resolves on its own; gentle washing and soft brushing can help. Baby Acne Baby acne is a common condition referenced by MedlinePlus (Mayo Clinic source). It typically appears as red bumps on the cheeks, nose, and forehead and usually clears without treatment. No special creams or washes are needed. Flat Head Syndrome (Positional Plagiocephaly) MedlinePlus references information from KidsHealth and the American Academy of Family Physicians on flat head syndrome. This can develop if a baby always sleeps with their head in the same position. Regular tummy time (when awake and supervised) helps prevent it, as does alternating the position of the baby's head during sleep. Colds and Respiratory Illness MedlinePlus lists common cold in babies as a common newborn/infant problem, referencing Mayo Clinic guidance. NHS resources cover colds, coughs, and ear infections in children. The FDA advises using caution with cough and cold medicines in children — many are not suitable for babies and young children. Parents should contact a healthcare provider if they are concerned. Fever in Babies The NHS states that a high temperature in a baby under 2 months is always serious and requires urgent medical attention. MedlinePlus provides guidance on fever in infants and children. The NHS advises that paracetamol should not be given to babies under 2 months, and ibuprofen should not be given to babies under 3 months or under 5 kg unless prescribed. Body temperature should be taken with a digital thermometer. Sources: MedlinePlus – Common Infant and Newborn Problems; NHS – Health (Colds, Coughs, Fever, Constipation); Mayo Clinic – Common Baby Rashes 10. When to Seek Medical Help Trust Your Instincts (NHS) The NHS emphasises: 'It can be difficult to tell when a baby or toddler is seriously ill, but the main thing is to trust your instincts. You know better than anyone else what your child is usually like, so you'll know when something is seriously wrong.' Warning Signs Requiring Urgent Medical Attention (NHS) Seek medical help urgently if your baby shows any of the following: • Temperature: A high temperature with cold feet and hands; a high temperature that does not come down with paracetamol or ibuprofen; a temperature in a baby under 2 months; your child is quiet and listless even when temperature is not high.

metadata.section: Table of Contents > 1. Feeding – Breastfeeding > (LAM). > 2. Feeding – Formula & Bottle Feeding > 3. Complementary Feeding & Weaning (Starting Solid Foods) > 4. Sleep > 5. Safe Sleep & SIDS Prevention > 6. Developmental Milestones > 7. Crying & Soothing Your Baby > 8. Hygiene & Physical Care > (NHS) > 9. Common Newborn Health Problems > 10. When to Seek Medical Help

content: to trust your instincts. You know better than anyone else what your child is usually like, so you'll know when something is seriously wrong.' Warning Signs Requiring Urgent Medical Attention (NHS) Seek medical help urgently if your baby shows any of the following: • Temperature: A high temperature with cold feet and hands; a high temperature that does not come down with paracetamol or ibuprofen; a temperature in a baby under 2 months; your child is quiet and listless even when temperature is not high. • Breathing: Rapid breathing or panting; a throaty noise while breathing; sucking the stomach in under the ribs; difficulty getting breath. • Skin: Blue, pale, blotchy, or ashen (grey) skin — on brown or black skin, check the palms of the hands or soles of the feet. • Consciousness: Hard to wake up, appears disoriented or confused. • Crying: Crying constantly and cannot be consoled; the cry does not sound like their normal cry. • Feeding: Not feeding normally and you are worried. • Urine: Drier nappies than usual — a sign of dehydration. • Vomiting: Green vomit. I CALL 999 FOR AN AMBULANCE IMMEDIATELY if your baby: stops breathing; will not wake up; has a spotty purple or red rash that does not fade when pressed with a glass (possible sepsis/meningitis); has a febrile seizure; has a severe allergic reaction; or is under 8 weeks old and you are very worried. How to Get Help (NHS) Monday to Friday during the day: call your GP surgery. Evenings and weekends: call NHS 111. For babies under 6 months, it is hard to assess over the phone — you can go to an urgent treatment centre or A&E if you are very worried. In an emergency, call 999 and do not attempt to drive yourself to A&E. Newborn Screening (NHS) Every newborn in the UK is offered a physical examination shortly after birth; a newborn blood spot test (heel prick) to screen for phenylketonuria (PKU), congenital hypothyroidism, sickle cell disease, cystic fibrosis, and MCADD (medium-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency); and a hearing screening test. These are part of routine NHS care and should be completed before discharge or within the first few weeks. Sources: NHS – Is Your Baby or Toddler Seriously Ill?; NHS – Newborn Screening; MedlinePlus – Common Infant and Newborn Problems 11. Bonding, Play & Development The Importance of Bonding Mayo Clinic states that the relationship between a caregiver and child is the foundation of a baby's development. Skin-to-skin contact — holding a baby naked or in just a nappy against skin — helps the newborn feel safe, secure, and loved, and supports brain development. MedlinePlus references guidance on 'Bonding with Your Newborn', and the NHS describes skin-to-skin time as a bonding experience that also helps with the first breastfeed. Holding a baby in response to cries does not spoil the baby — Mayo Clinic confirms that responding quickly builds a strong bond and provides the security the baby needs to

metadata.section: Table of Contents > 1. Feeding – Breastfeeding > (LAM). > 2. Feeding – Formula & Bottle Feeding > 3. Complementary Feeding & Weaning (Starting Solid Foods) > 4. Sleep > 5. Safe Sleep & SIDS Prevention > 6. Developmental Milestones > 7. Crying & Soothing Your Baby > 8. Hygiene & Physical Care > (NHS) > 9. Common Newborn Health Problems > 10. When to Seek Medical Help > 11. Bonding, Play & Development

content: development. Skin-to-skin contact — holding a baby naked or in just a nappy against skin — helps the newborn feel safe, secure, and loved, and supports brain development. MedlinePlus references guidance on 'Bonding with Your Newborn', and the NHS describes skin-to-skin time as a bonding experience that also helps with the first breastfeed. Holding a baby in response to cries does not spoil the baby — Mayo Clinic confirms that responding quickly builds a strong bond and provides the security the baby needs to eventually settle without help. Skin-to-Skin and Tummy Time MedlinePlus references NICHD guidance on tummy time for a healthy baby. Tummy time — placing a baby on their tummy while awake and supervised — strengthens neck, shoulder, and arm muscles, supports motor development, and helps prevent flat head syndrome (positional plagiocephaly). Many newborns initially dislike tummy time; begin with brief sessions and gradually increase duration. Tummy time should only happen when the baby is awake and being watched — always place babies on their back to sleep. Talking, Reading & Singing (Mayo Clinic) Simple conversation lays the groundwork for language development. Mayo Clinic advises making eye contact when the baby looks at you, talking, singing, and reading out loud. Reacting to a baby's coos and gurgles encourages communication. The NHS 'Baby's Development – Play and Learning' section provides baby and toddler play ideas and tips on how to support speech development. Bedtime stories from an early age support literacy. Mayo Clinic's guidance on milestones 10–12 months advises using adult speech (not baby talk) so the child can learn to imitate words correctly, and speaking all the languages the family uses. Stimulation Without Over-Stimulation Providing toys with different textures or bold patterns can stimulate a baby's developing senses. However, Mayo Clinic warns that too much stimulation can overwhelm a baby. Signs of overstimulation include crying, turning away, or arching the back. Limiting stimulation before sleep and winding down with calm activities (such as reading or quiet talking) helps babies settle. The NHS recommends limiting distractions during mealtimes and avoiding screens at mealtimes. Teething (NHS) Teething typically begins around 6 months of age, though it can start earlier or later. Signs include drooling, gnawing on objects, swollen gums, and irritability. The NHS states that cooling teething rings, rubbing gums with a clean finger, and teething gels may provide relief. Brushing should begin as soon as the first tooth appears. Note that teething discomfort can temporarily disturb sleep patterns. Sources: NHS – Baby's Development; Mayo Clinic – Infant Development Birth–3 Months; Mayo Clinic – Milestones 10–12 Months; MedlinePlus – Bonding with Your Newborn; MedlinePlus – Infant and Newborn Development 12. Vaccinations Vaccinations are a critical component of infant health. The NHS advises that keeping vaccinations up to date protects against childhood illnesses that may also increase the risk of SIDS. Mayo Clinic provides detailed guidance on childhood vaccines, addressing common parent questions and concerns. MedlinePlus (referencing the CDC) confirms that following the recommended vaccination schedule is important for child health.

Safe Sleep & SIDS Prevention > 6. Developmental Milestones > 7. Crying & Soothing Your Baby > 8. Hygiene & Physical Care > (NHS) > 9. Common Newborn Health Problems > 10. When to Seek Medical Help > 11. Bonding, Play & Development > 12. Vaccinations

content: Sources: NHS – Baby's Development; Mayo Clinic – Infant Development Birth–3 Months; Mayo Clinic – Milestones 10–12 Months; MedlinePlus – Bonding with Your Newborn; MedlinePlus – Infant and Newborn Development 12. Vaccinations Vaccinations are a critical component of infant health. The NHS advises that keeping vaccinations up to date protects against childhood illnesses that may also increase the risk of SIDS. Mayo Clinic provides detailed guidance on childhood vaccines, addressing common parent questions and concerns. MedlinePlus (referencing the CDC) confirms that following the recommended vaccination schedule is important for child health. UK Routine Vaccination Schedule (NHS) In the UK, routine vaccinations begin at 8 weeks. Common vaccines in the first year include those against diphtheria, tetanus, pertussis (whooping cough), polio, Hib (Haemophilus influenzae type b), hepatitis B, meningitis B, rotavirus, and pneumococcal disease. Flu vaccination is recommended for eligible infants. The NHS website (Vaccine Schedule for Children 0 to 5) provides full details of what is given at each age and what each vaccine protects against. Why Vaccinations Matter Vaccines protect individual babies from serious, sometimes life-threatening diseases and also contribute to community immunity, protecting those who cannot be vaccinated (such as very young or immunocompromised individuals). Mayo Clinic notes that flu vaccination is particularly important for infants, and that it can be doubly important in certain seasons. Concerns about vaccine safety should be raised with a healthcare provider — vaccines undergo rigorous testing before approval. Sources: NHS – Health (Vaccine Schedule for Children 0–5); NHS – SIDS (vaccination mention); Mayo Clinic – Childhood Vaccines: Tough Questions, Straight Answers; MedlinePlus – Infant and Newborn Development (vaccine reference) 13. Nutrition, Vitamins & Supplements Vitamin D The NHS and Mayo Clinic both highlight Vitamin D as important for infants. The NHS recommends that breastfed babies, or babies having less than 500 ml of formula a day, should be given a daily supplement of 8.5 to 10 micrograms (mcg) of Vitamin D from birth. Breastfed babies should also receive Vitamins A and C from 6 to 12 months. Babies having 500 ml or more of formula per day do not need supplements, as formula is already fortified. Mayo Clinic advises asking the baby's healthcare provider about Vitamin D supplements, especially when breastfeeding, as breast milk may not provide enough — Vitamin D is essential for absorbing the nutrients needed for strong bones. Iron MedlinePlus references Mayo Clinic guidance on iron deficiency prevention in children. Breastfed babies may need iron supplements after 4 months if no iron-fortified solid foods have been introduced. Formula-fed babies typically receive iron through fortified formula. Once solid foods are introduced (around 6 months), iron-rich foods such as meat, fish, dark green vegetables, beans, lentils, and fortified breakfast cereals are beneficial. The NHS advises trying to include iron-containing foods when transitioning from 7 months onwards. Children's Vitamins (NHS) All children aged 1 to 4 years should be given daily vitamin supplements containing Vitamins A, C, and D.

metadata.section: Table of Contents > 1. Feeding – Breastfeeding > (LAM). > 2. Feeding – Formula & Bottle Feeding > 3. Complementary Feeding & Weaning (Starting Solid Foods) > 4. Sleep > 5. Safe Sleep & SIDS Prevention > 6. Developmental Milestones > 7. Crying & Soothing Your Baby > 8. Hygiene & Physical Care > (NHS) > 9. Common Newborn Health Problems > 10. When to Seek Medical Help > 11. Bonding, Play & Development > 12. Vaccinations > 13. Nutrition, Vitamins & Supplements

content: Iron MedlinePlus references Mayo Clinic guidance on iron deficiency prevention in children. Breastfed babies may need iron supplements after 4 months if no iron-fortified solid foods have been introduced. Formula-fed babies typically receive iron through fortified formula. Once solid foods are introduced (around 6 months), iron-rich foods such as meat, fish, dark green vegetables, beans, lentils, and fortified breakfast cereals are beneficial. The NHS advises trying to include iron-containing foods when transitioning from 7 months onwards. Children's Vitamins (NHS) All children aged 1 to 4 years should be given daily vitamin supplements containing Vitamins A, C, and D. The NHS website provides further detail under 'Vitamins for Children' in the Weaning and Feeding section. Global Context: Undernutrition (WHO) WHO reports that undernutrition is associated with approximately 45% of all child deaths globally — estimated at 2.7 million deaths annually. Globally in 2022, 149 million children under 5 were estimated to be stunted (too short for age), 45 million were wasted (too thin for height), and 37 million were overweight or obese. Few children receive nutritionally adequate and safe complementary foods; in many countries fewer than a quarter of infants 6–23 months meet criteria for dietary diversity and appropriate feeding frequency. The first 2 years of a child's life are particularly critical — optimal nutrition during this period lowers morbidity and mortality, reduces the risk of chronic disease, and fosters better development overall. Sources: NHS – Your Baby's First Solid Foods (vitamins section); NHS – Vitamins for Children; Mayo Clinic – Feeding Your Newborn (Vitamin D); MedlinePlus – Iron Deficiency in Children; WHO – Infant and Young Child Feeding Fact Sheet 14. Parental & Caregiver Wellbeing It Is Normal to Feel Overwhelmed Mayo Clinic and the NHS both acknowledge the reality of caregiver exhaustion. Mayo Clinic states that tired caregivers can find it hard to focus, remember details, or find solutions to problems. New caregivers commonly experience frequently changing emotions in the first month. The NHS advises parents to 'sleep when your baby sleeps' where possible, set aside non-essential tasks, and accept that everything else can wait. Mayo Clinic's guidance on crying babies emphasises that babies often cry most in the first couple of months — it does get better. Self-Care Strategies Mayo Clinic recommends: taking breaks and resting when you can; getting enough sleep; doing things you enjoy when time allows; and making a little time each day for activities that help you relax, as a more relaxed caregiver finds it easier to calm the baby. Infants can sense tension in adults and may react by crying. Sharing night feeds and early-morning routines with a partner or trusted helper is strongly recommended by the NHS. Asking for Help Mayo Clinic advises asking a partner, loved ones, and friends for support. Let them know what help is needed — errands, chores, cooking, or caring for older children. The NHS describes a wide network of available support including midwives, health visitors, GPs, breastfeeding drop-in groups, and mother

content: relaxed caregiver finds it easier to calm the baby. Infants can sense tension in adults and may react by crying. Sharing night feeds and early-morning routines with a partner or trusted helper is strongly recommended by the NHS. Asking for Help Mayo Clinic advises asking a partner, loved ones, and friends for support. Let them know what help is needed — errands, chores, cooking, or caring for older children. The NHS describes a wide network of available support including midwives, health visitors, GPs, breastfeeding drop-in groups, and mother support groups. MedlinePlus includes a section specifically for new dads with tips on managing stress. Postnatal Depression Both Mayo Clinic and the NHS advise calling a healthcare provider if you feel severely sad or if sadness persists for more than a few weeks — this may indicate postnatal depression, which is treatable. It is important not to ignore these feelings. Support for Persistent Crying Babies (NHS) The NHS advises parents struggling with a persistently crying baby to contact the Cry-sis helpline (UK): 0800 448 0737, open 9am–10pm, 7 days a week. Cry-sis can provide guidance and connect parents with others who have been in the same situation. A health visitor or GP can also be consulted, and keeping a record of when and how long the baby cries can help identify patterns. Twins and Multiples (NHS) The NHS 'Newborn Twins and Multiples' section provides specific feeding advice for families with twins or multiples. Parents of multiples face additional physical and emotional demands, and seeking extra support is especially important. Twins are also at higher risk of SIDS due to increased likelihood of premature birth or low birth weight — extra attention to safe sleep arrangements is essential. Sources: NHS – Baby Support and Services; NHS – Soothing a Crying Baby; Mayo Clinic – Crying Baby; Mayo Clinic – Newborn Health; MedlinePlus – New Dad: Tips to Help Manage Stress This knowledge base was compiled from publicly available health information published by the World Health Organization (WHO), the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), the UK National Health Service (NHS), MedlinePlus (U.S. National Library of Medicine / NIH), and Mayo Clinic. It is intended for informational and RAG-database purposes only. Always consult a qualified healthcare professional for individual medical advice regarding your baby's health.
